title: Tracking AI narratives — seeing META collapse and NVDA become the only positive anchor
author: topicspace
selftext:
I’ve been experimenting with a way to track how AI-related narratives actually move across companies (instead of just following news headlines).

Pulled a fresh batch of data last night (\~450 new events), and something pretty unusual showed up:

\- Meta went from strongly positive to strongly negative momentum in a single window (largest reversal I’ve seen so far)

\- NVIDIA flipped positive and is now basically the only actor with both momentum + acceleration

\- At the same time, AMZN / GOOGL / TSLA all rolled over into negative

So instead of “AI momentum continuing,” it looks more like:

→ the system just reset  

→ one anchor (NVDA) + broad narrative unwind  

Curious how people here think about this kind of signal vs just price action:

Do you see narrative-level shifts like this show up before market moves, or mostly as confirmation?

I threw a simple public version of what I’m building here if helpful context:

https://topicspace.com/

Would genuinely love a sanity check—still figuring out if this is useful or just interesting.

comments.author: Patient_Kangaroo4864
comments.body: 450 events is basically noise unless you’re weighting by impact and source quality. NVDA staying positive while others wobble just means the market still sees them as the shovel seller, not a narrative collapse.

replies.author: topicspace
replies.body:
Yeah that’s a fair push.

It’s not just raw event count — everything is weighted by source, recency, and how narratives propagate across actors. The interesting part isn’t that NVDA is strong (that’s obvious), it’s how quickly it flipped from negative to positive while others reversed at the same time.

That kind of synchronized shift tends to show up before the broader story becomes consensus.

Still early, but that’s the behavior I’m trying to track.

comments.author: sagesofthe4th_world
comments.body:
Respectfully - I see the AI bubble bursting in a very major and obvious way. 

We are headed for a recession bub. Better pack it up. 

  
None of these companies will be able to avoid it. And the technical debt will keep engineers employed for years to come.
